solar.hint: Our Solar System. Click any object to learn about it. Use the scale slider to see how empty space really is.

sun.name: The Sun
sun.caption: A medium-sized yellow star at the centre of our Solar System. Its gravity holds all the planets in orbit.

mercury.name: Mercury
mercury.caption: The smallest planet and the closest to the Sun. It has almost no atmosphere, so it bakes by day and freezes at night.

venus.name: Venus
venus.caption: Earth's 'evil twin'. A thick CO₂ atmosphere traps heat, making it the hottest planet — even hotter than Mercury.

earth.name: Earth
earth.caption: The only planet known to have life. Liquid water, a protective atmosphere and a magnetic field make it special.

moon.name: The Moon (Luna)
moon.caption: Earth's only natural satellite. Its gravity causes our ocean tides. The same side always faces Earth.

mars.name: Mars
mars.caption: The 'Red Planet'. Its colour comes from iron oxide (rust) on the surface. Has the tallest volcano in the Solar System.

jupiter.name: Jupiter
jupiter.caption: The biggest planet — a giant ball of gas with a storm (the Great Red Spot) larger than Earth that has lasted centuries.

saturn.name: Saturn
saturn.caption: Famous for its spectacular rings made of ice and rock. Saturn is so light it would float in water!

uranus.name: Uranus
uranus.caption: An 'ice giant' that rotates on its side. A long-ago collision is thought to have knocked it over.

neptune.name: Neptune
neptune.caption: The windiest planet — supersonic winds rip through its blue atmosphere at over 2,000 km/h.

asteroid.name: Asteroid Belt
asteroid.caption: Rocky leftovers from the Solar System's formation, mostly between Mars and Jupiter. Smaller pieces that fly through space are called meteoroids.

comet.name: Comet
comet.caption: A 'dirty snowball' of ice, dust and rock. When it nears the Sun, it heats up and grows a glowing tail that always points away from the Sun.

facts.Tail: Up to millions of km long

meteor.name: Meteor (Shooting Star)
meteor.caption: A meteoroid burning up in Earth's atmosphere from friction. If a piece reaches the ground it's called a meteorite.

stars.hint: Stars come in many sizes and colours. A star's colour tells you its temperature — blue is hottest, red is coolest.

red-dwarf.name: Red Dwarf
red-dwarf.caption: The most common type of star. Small, cool and very long-lived — they can shine for trillions of years.

sun-like.name: Yellow Dwarf (Sun-like)
sun-like.caption: A medium star like our Sun. Burns hydrogen for about 10 billion years before swelling into a red giant.

blue-giant.name: Blue Giant
blue-giant.caption: Massive, extremely hot and short-lived. They burn through their fuel fast and often end their lives in a supernova.

red-giant.name: Red Giant
red-giant.caption: What stars like our Sun become near the end of their life — they swell up huge and cool. Our Sun will swallow Mercury and Venus when it becomes one.

remnants.hint: When stars die, they leave behind some of the strangest objects in the universe.

white-dwarf.name: White Dwarf
white-dwarf.caption: The hot, dense core left behind when a Sun-like star dies. Earth-sized but as heavy as the Sun.

neutron-star.name: Neutron Star
neutron-star.caption: What's left after a massive star explodes. A city-sized ball so dense that one teaspoon would weigh as much as a mountain.

pulsar.name: Pulsar
pulsar.caption: A spinning neutron star that beams radio waves out of its poles. Like a cosmic lighthouse, we see a pulse every time the beam sweeps past Earth.

black-hole.name: Black Hole
black-hole.caption: An object with gravity so strong that not even light can escape. The bright ring around it is an accretion disk — superheated gas spiralling in.

supernova.name: Supernova
supernova.caption: The gigantic explosion of a dying massive star. Briefly outshines an entire galaxy and scatters elements like iron and gold across space.

galaxies.hint: Galaxies are vast collections of stars, gas, dust and dark matter held together by gravity. There are over 2 trillion in the observable universe.

milky-way.name: Milky Way
milky-way.caption: Our home galaxy. A barred spiral containing 100–400 billion stars. Our Sun is on one of the spiral arms.

andromeda.name: Andromeda
andromeda.caption: Our closest large galactic neighbour. It's heading toward us — in about 4.5 billion years it will collide with the Milky Way.

elliptical.name: Elliptical Galaxy
elliptical.caption: Football-shaped galaxies made of mostly older stars, with little gas left to form new ones. Often the largest galaxies in the universe.

irregular.name: Irregular Galaxy
irregular.caption: Galaxies with no clear shape, often warped by collisions with other galaxies. Many small galaxies are irregular.

constellations.hint: Constellations are patterns of stars seen from Earth. Drag to rotate the night sky. Many of these are visible from Western Australia!

southern-cross.name: Southern Cross (Crux)
southern-cross.caption: The most famous constellation of the southern sky — it appears on the Australian flag. Used by sailors to find south.

facts.Cultural: On flags of Australia, NZ, Brazil

orion.name: Orion (The Hunter)
orion.caption: Visible worldwide. Look for the three bright 'belt' stars in a row. Contains the red supergiant Betelgeuse.

scorpius.name: Scorpius (The Scorpion)
scorpius.caption: A bright, scorpion-shaped pattern with the red star Antares as its heart. Best viewed from the Southern Hemisphere.
